title: Normal Operation
shortDescription: One blink indicates the water heater is operating normally with no faults.
fullDescription: A single status LED blink on a Rheem water heater control board indicates normal operation. The burner may or may not be active depending on water temperature demand.
safetyWarning: Water heaters involve gas, high temperatures, and pressure. If you smell gas, leave immediately and call your gas company.

fixSteps.title: Verify Hot Water
fixSteps.instruction: Run a hot water faucet to confirm hot water is available.

fixSteps.title: Check Temperature
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the temperature dial is set to your desired setting (120°F recommended).

fixSteps.title: Listen for Burner
fixSteps.instruction: If hot water is being used, you should hear the burner ignite.

fixSteps.title: Check Pilot
fixSteps.instruction: Look through the viewport to confirm the pilot light is lit (if applicable).

whenToCallPro: If hot water is not available despite normal status indication.

faq.q: Is 1 blink an error?
faq.a: No, 1 blink means normal operation on Rheem water heaters.

faq.q: What temperature should I set?
faq.a: 120°F is recommended for safety and energy efficiency.

faq.q: How long does recovery take?
faq.a: A 40-gallon gas water heater recovers in about 30-40 minutes.

title: Thermopile Voltage Low
shortDescription: The thermopile is not generating enough voltage to keep the gas valve open.
fullDescription: Two blinks on a Rheem water heater indicate the thermopile (or thermogenerator) voltage is below the minimum threshold needed to power the gas valve. The pilot may stay lit but the main burner will not fire.
safetyWarning: If you smell gas at any point, leave the area and call your gas company. Do not attempt to light the pilot if there is a strong gas odor.
symptoms: Pilot lights but main burner does not fire

fixSteps.title: Check Pilot Flame
fixSteps.instruction: Look through the viewport — the pilot should produce a strong blue flame touching the thermopile.

fixSteps.title: Clean Thermopile
fixSteps.instruction: Clean the thermopile tip with fine sandpaper or emery cloth.

fixSteps.title: Check Connection
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the thermopile wire is firmly connected to the gas valve.

fixSteps.title: Test Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Measure DC voltage across thermopile leads — should be 400mV+ with pilot lit.

whenToCallPro: If cleaning does not restore voltage above 400mV — thermopile needs replacement.

faq.q: What is a thermopile?
faq.a: A device that generates a small voltage from the pilot flame heat to power the gas valve.

faq.q: How much voltage is needed?
faq.a: Minimum 400mV; a new thermopile produces 650-850mV.

faq.q: How much does a thermopile cost?
faq.a: $15-$30 for the part.

title: Pressure Switch Error
shortDescription: The pressure switch for power-vent models failed to close or opened during operation.
fullDescription: Three blinks indicate the pressure switch on a power-vent Rheem water heater did not close properly. This safety device ensures the exhaust blower is creating adequate draft before allowing gas flow.
safetyWarning: Never bypass the pressure switch — it prevents carbon monoxide buildup in your home.
symptoms: Blower runs but burner does not light

fixSteps.title: Check Vent
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the vent pipe for blockages — bird nests, ice, or debris.

fixSteps.title: Check Hose
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the pressure switch rubber hose is connected and not cracked.

fixSteps.title: Test Switch
fixSteps.instruction: With blower running, check pressure switch for continuity.

fixSteps.title: Check Blower
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the exhaust blower starts when heating is called.

whenToCallPro: If the blower is not running or the vent is clear but the switch still fails.

faq.q: Does my water heater have a pressure switch?
faq.a: Only power-vent (electrically vented) models have pressure switches.

faq.q: Can I bypass the switch to test?
faq.a: Only momentarily to diagnose — never leave it bypassed.

faq.q: How much is a pressure switch?
faq.a: $20-$50 for the part.

title: High Temperature Shutdown
shortDescription: The water temperature exceeded safe limits and the ECO (Energy Cutoff) switch tripped.
fullDescription: Four blinks indicate the ECO (high temperature limit) switch has tripped because the water temperature exceeded approximately 180°F. This is a critical safety feature to prevent scalding and tank failure.
safetyWarning: CRITICAL: Water above 150°F can cause severe scalding in seconds. Do not use hot water until this is resolved. The ECO may indicate a failing gas valve that is stuck open.

fixSteps.title: Turn Off Gas
fixSteps.instruction: Set the gas valve to OFF immediately.

fixSteps.title: Let Cool
fixSteps.instruction: Allow the water to cool before using any hot water faucets.

fixSteps.title: Reset ECO
fixSteps.instruction: Press the red reset button on the thermostat (behind the access panel).

fixSteps.title: Monitor
fixSteps.instruction: If the ECO trips again, the gas valve likely needs replacement.

whenToCallPro: If the ECO trips again after reset — gas valve replacement is needed.

faq.q: What is the ECO switch?
faq.a: The Energy Cutoff switch shuts off gas at approximately 180°F to prevent dangerously hot water.

faq.q: Is this dangerous?
faq.a: Yes, water over 150°F can cause severe scalding burns.

faq.q: How much is a gas valve?
faq.a: $150-$350 installed by a professional.

title: Sensor Failure
shortDescription: The water temperature sensor has failed or is reading out of range.
fullDescription: Five blinks on a Rheem water heater indicate the temperature sensor (thermistor) is reading an abnormal value. The control board cannot regulate water temperature without accurate sensor data.
safetyWarning: A failed sensor may allow the water to overheat. Do not use hot water until the issue is diagnosed.
symptoms: Water too hot or too cold

fixSteps.title: Check Connection
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the sensor wire is plugged in securely at the control board.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Wire
fixSteps.instruction: Look for damage, corrosion, or water exposure on the sensor wire.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Measure resistance across the sensor leads — compare to specification.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install a new temperature sensor matching the OEM specification.

whenToCallPro: If replacing the sensor does not resolve the error.

faq.q: Where is the sensor?
faq.a: Typically mounted in the lower portion of the tank near the burner assembly.

faq.q: What resistance should the sensor read?
faq.a: Check your model's service manual — values vary by model and temperature.

faq.q: Can I use a generic sensor?
faq.a: Use an OEM replacement to ensure proper resistance curve matching.

title: Gas Control Valve Fault
shortDescription: The electronic gas control valve has an internal fault.
fullDescription: Seven blinks indicate the gas control valve has detected an internal electronic or mechanical failure. The valve will not allow gas flow in this condition.
safetyWarning: Do not attempt to repair or bypass the gas valve. If you smell gas, evacuate and call your gas company.
symptoms: Pilot may or may not light

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Turn the gas valve to OFF, wait 60 seconds, then turn to PILOT position.

fixSteps.title: Check for Gas Smell
fixSteps.instruction: If you smell gas, evacuate and call the gas company.

fixSteps.title: Attempt Re-light
fixSteps.instruction: Try to relight the pilot following the instructions on the water heater label.

fixSteps.title: Replace Valve
fixSteps.instruction: If the error persists, the gas control valve assembly must be replaced.

whenToCallPro: After a power cycle fails to resolve the error — gas valve replacement is needed.

faq.q: Is it worth replacing the gas valve?
faq.a: If the tank is under 8 years old, yes. Older tanks may be better replaced entirely.

faq.q: Can I replace the gas valve myself?
faq.a: Gas valve replacement should be done by a licensed plumber or gas technician.

faq.q: How much does a gas valve cost?
faq.a: $150-$300 for the part, plus $100-$200 labor.

title: Electronic Ignition Failure
shortDescription: The electronic ignition system failed to light the pilot or main burner.
fullDescription: Eight blinks indicate the electronic spark ignition system was unable to ignite the pilot or main burner within the allotted number of attempts. This applies to models with electronic ignition (no standing pilot).
safetyWarning: If you smell gas after failed ignition attempts, leave the area immediately and call your gas company.

fixSteps.title: Check Gas Supply
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the gas shutoff valve is open and other gas appliances work.

fixSteps.title: Clean Igniter
fixSteps.instruction: Clean the spark igniter electrode with fine sandpaper.

fixSteps.title: Check Gap
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the spark gap between electrode and ground is approximately 1/8 inch.

fixSteps.title: Reset
fixSteps.instruction: Turn gas valve OFF for 60 seconds, then restart the ignition sequence.

whenToCallPro: If cleaning the igniter and verifying gas supply does not resolve ignition failure.

faq.q: Does my water heater have electronic ignition?
faq.a: Newer models use electronic spark ignition — no standing pilot light.

faq.q: Can I hear the igniter?
faq.a: Yes, you should hear a clicking sound when the system attempts ignition.

faq.q: How often do igniters fail?
faq.a: Igniters typically last 5-10 years.

title: Flammable Vapor Sensor (FV) Lockout
shortDescription: The flammable vapor sensor has detected combustible vapors near the water heater.
fullDescription: FV lockout occurs when the flammable vapor sensor (FVIR) detects the presence of flammable vapors near the water heater burner. This is a critical safety feature that prevents the water heater from igniting vapors that could cause an explosion.
safetyWarning: CRITICAL SAFETY WARNING: Flammable vapors can cause explosions. Do not use any ignition sources. Ventilate the area immediately. If you suspect a gas leak, evacuate and call 911.

causes.cause: Gasoline or solvent fumes near water heater
Paint, adhesive, or cleaning product vapors
Natural gas leak near water heater

symptoms: Status light not blinking or rapid blinking

fixSteps.title: Ventilate
fixSteps.instruction: Open doors and windows to ventilate the area around the water heater.

fixSteps.title: Remove Sources
fixSteps.instruction: Remove any gasoline, paint, solvents, or chemicals from the water heater area.

fixSteps.title: Wait
fixSteps.instruction: Allow the area to ventilate for at least 30 minutes before attempting reset.

fixSteps.title: Reset
fixSteps.instruction: After ventilation, turn gas valve OFF for 60 seconds, then relight following label instructions.

whenToCallPro: If the sensor trips with no obvious vapor source — the sensor itself may be faulty.

faq.q: What triggers the FV sensor?
faq.a: Gasoline, paint thinner, solvents, adhesives, and other flammable chemical vapors.

faq.q: Can I store gas cans near my water heater?
faq.a: Absolutely not. Keep all flammable liquids at least 10 feet from the water heater.

faq.q: How much does a new FV sensor cost?
faq.a: $50-$150 for the part. Some models have replaceable sensors, others require the full burner assembly.

title: Anode Rod Depletion Warning
shortDescription: The sacrificial anode rod is depleted and the tank is at risk of corrosion.
fullDescription: Some Rheem water heaters with electronic controls include an anode rod depletion alert. This warning means the magnesium or aluminum anode rod that protects the tank from corrosion is nearly consumed and should be replaced.
safetyWarning: Turn off the gas and water supply before replacing the anode rod. Hot water can cause burns.

causes.cause: High water usage depleting anode faster

symptoms: Rotten egg smell in hot water

fixSteps.title: Turn Off
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the gas and cold water supply to the water heater.

fixSteps.title: Drain Partially
fixSteps.instruction: Drain a few gallons from the tank to reduce weight on the anode.

fixSteps.title: Remove Old Rod
fixSteps.instruction: Use a 1-1/16 inch socket to remove the anode rod from the top of the tank.

fixSteps.title: Install New Rod
fixSteps.instruction: Thread in a new magnesium anode rod and tighten. Apply Teflon tape to threads.

whenToCallPro: If the anode is stuck or you cannot access it — a plumber can use specialized tools.

faq.q: How often should I replace the anode?
faq.a: Every 3-5 years for standard rods; powered anodes last longer.

faq.q: What does the anode rod do?
faq.a: It sacrificially corrodes to protect the steel tank from rusting.

faq.q: Can I use an aluminum rod instead?
faq.a: Yes, but magnesium provides better protection. Use aluminum if you have very hard water.

title: No Power / Pilot Out
shortDescription: No status LED is visible — the pilot is out or control has no power.
fullDescription: No LED on a Rheem water heater means the gas control valve has no power, which typically means the pilot light is out. Without the pilot, the thermopile generates no voltage to power the control electronics.
safetyWarning: If you smell gas when the pilot is out, do not attempt to relight. Leave the area and call your gas company.

fixSteps.title: Check Gas
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the gas shutoff valve is in the ON position (handle parallel to pipe).

fixSteps.title: Smell Check
fixSteps.instruction: Sniff near the water heater for gas odor. If you smell gas, leave and call gas company.

fixSteps.title: Relight Pilot
fixSteps.instruction: Follow the lighting instructions printed on the water heater label.

fixSteps.title: Hold and Test
fixSteps.instruction: Hold the pilot button for 60 seconds after lighting. If it goes out, the thermopile is weak.

whenToCallPro: If the pilot will not stay lit after multiple attempts.

faq.q: Why did my pilot go out?
faq.a: Drafts, a dirty pilot orifice, or a failing thermopile are common causes.

faq.q: Is it dangerous to relight?
faq.a: No, as long as you follow the instructions and do not smell gas.

faq.q: How long to hold the pilot button?
faq.a: Hold for 60 seconds to allow the thermopile to heat up and generate voltage.

title: Ignition Failure
shortDescription: The unit failed to ignite after the maximum number of attempts.
fullDescription:
Error E1 on Rheem tankless water heaters indicates the unit attempted to ignite the burner but was unable to detect a flame. The ignition sequence fires the spark igniter while opening the gas valve, and if no flame is detected within the allowed time, the unit shuts down with this error.

Common causes include a closed gas supply valve, insufficient gas pressure, a dirty igniter electrode, or a failed flame detection rod. On propane models, a low tank level is a frequent cause.

If the unit has been sitting idle for an extended period, air may have entered the gas line, requiring several ignition attempts to purge.
safetyWarning: This error involves gas ignition. If you smell gas, evacuate the area immediately and call your gas company. Do not attempt to manually light the burner.

fixSteps.title: Check Gas Supply
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the gas shutoff valve to the unit is fully open. Check that other gas appliances work.

fixSteps.title: Reset the Unit
fixSteps.instruction: Turn the unit off, wait 30 seconds, then turn it back on to clear the lockout.

fixSteps.title: Clean Igniter and Flame Rod
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the front cover and clean the igniter electrode and flame rod with fine sandpaper.

fixSteps.title: Check Gas Pressure
fixSteps.instruction: Measure inlet gas pressure with a manometer while the unit attempts to fire.

whenToCallPro: If gas supply is confirmed and igniter cleaning does not resolve the issue, call a licensed plumber to check gas pressure and internal components.

faq.q: How do I reset my Rheem after E1?
faq.a: Turn the unit off using the power button, wait 30 seconds, then turn it back on.

faq.q: Is E1 dangerous?
faq.a: The unit safely shuts down, but if you smell gas, evacuate and call your gas company immediately.

title: Flame Loss During Operation
shortDescription: The flame was detected at ignition but lost during burner operation.
fullDescription:
Error E2 on Rheem tankless water heaters indicates the unit ignited successfully but the flame was lost while the burner was operating. The flame detection rod stopped sensing a flame signal during the heating cycle.

This is commonly caused by a dirty flame rod, gas pressure fluctuations, or wind affecting the venting system. If this occurs during heavy hot water use, the gas line may be undersized and unable to maintain pressure.

Intermittent E2 errors during high-demand periods may indicate multiple gas appliances are competing for gas supply, causing pressure drops.
safetyWarning: Flame loss during operation may indicate gas supply instability. If you smell gas, shut off the supply immediately and call your gas company.
symptoms: Unit restarts after a brief pause

fixSteps.title: Clean Flame Rod
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the front cover and clean the flame detection rod with fine sandpaper or emery cloth.

fixSteps.title: Check Condensate Drain
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the condensate drain is not clogged and condensate is not backing up onto the burner.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Venting
fixSteps.instruction: Check the vent termination for wind exposure and verify all joints are sealed.

fixSteps.title: Check Gas Pressure Under Load
fixSteps.instruction: Have a technician measure gas pressure while the unit fires at full capacity.

whenToCallPro: If flame rod cleaning does not resolve the issue, a technician should perform a combustion analysis and gas pressure test.

faq.q: Why does my hot water go cold mid-shower?
faq.a: E2 causes the unit to shut down when the flame is lost. Cleaning the flame rod is the most common fix.

faq.q: How often should the flame rod be cleaned?
faq.a: At least once a year during annual maintenance.

title: Exhaust Temperature Limit Exceeded
shortDescription: The exhaust gas temperature has exceeded the safe operating limit.
fullDescription:
Error E3 on Rheem tankless water heaters indicates the exhaust temperature sensor has detected temperatures above the maximum safe threshold. High exhaust temperatures indicate heat is not being transferred efficiently from the combustion gases to the water.

The most common cause is scale buildup on the heat exchanger, which insulates the heat transfer surfaces and forces more heat to exit through the exhaust. A blocked or restricted vent can also cause exhaust temperatures to rise.

This error should be taken seriously as it may indicate inefficient combustion that could produce elevated carbon monoxide levels.
safetyWarning: High exhaust temperatures may indicate incomplete combustion and elevated CO levels. Ensure CO detectors are functioning. Do not override this safety shutdown.

fixSteps.title: Check Exhaust Vent
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the exhaust vent termination and full vent run for blockages or restrictions.

fixSteps.title: Check Air Intake
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the combustion air intake is not blocked.

fixSteps.title: Descale Heat Exchanger
fixSteps.instruction: Flush the heat exchanger with descaling solution using the isolation valves and a pump for 45-60 minutes.

fixSteps.title: Reset and Monitor
fixSteps.instruction: After service, reset the unit and monitor operation to confirm the error does not return.

whenToCallPro: If the vent is clear and descaling does not resolve the issue, a technician should perform a combustion analysis.

faq.q: Can a dirty heat exchanger cause E3?
faq.a: Yes. Scale reduces heat transfer, forcing more heat through the exhaust.

faq.q: How often should I descale?
faq.a: Annually in hard water areas, every 2-3 years in soft water areas.

title: Overheating - Outlet Temperature Too High
shortDescription: The outlet water temperature has exceeded the safe limit.
fullDescription:
Error E4 indicates the outlet water temperature has exceeded the maximum safe limit, typically around 185 degrees Fahrenheit. The unit shuts down to prevent scalding and heat exchanger damage.

Low water flow is the primary cause. When water flows too slowly through the heat exchanger, it absorbs excessive heat. This is commonly caused by a clogged inlet filter, partially closed valves, or scale buildup restricting the water path.

Repeated E4 errors can permanently damage the heat exchanger and should be resolved promptly.
safetyWarning: CRITICAL: Water above 150 degrees Fahrenheit causes severe scalding in seconds. Do not use hot water until this is resolved.

fixSteps.title: Check Water Valves
fixSteps.instruction: Verify both inlet and outlet isolation valves are fully open.

fixSteps.title: Clean Inlet Filter
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and clean the inlet water filter screen of sediment and debris.

fixSteps.title: Descale Heat Exchanger
fixSteps.instruction: Flush the heat exchanger with descaling solution for 45-60 minutes.

fixSteps.title: Measure Flow Rate
fixSteps.instruction: After descaling, measure hot water flow rate to confirm it meets the minimum activation flow.

whenToCallPro: If descaling and filter cleaning do not resolve the overheating, a technician should check the flow control valve.

faq.q: How do I prevent E4?
faq.a: Regular annual maintenance including descaling and filter cleaning prevents most overheating issues.

faq.q: Can I use the unit after E4 clears?
faq.a: Yes, once the cause is addressed and the unit resets. Monitor water temperature carefully.

title: Overheating - Heat Exchanger Temperature Limit
shortDescription: The heat exchanger has exceeded its maximum safe temperature.
fullDescription:
Error E5 indicates the heat exchanger body temperature has exceeded the safe operating limit. This is a more severe overheating condition than E4 (outlet water temperature) because it means the heat exchanger material itself is at risk of damage.

Severe scale buildup, extremely low water flow, or a failed gas modulation valve stuck at high fire can cause E5. Repeated E5 events can warp or crack the heat exchanger, leading to expensive repairs.

This error takes priority over other errors and the unit will not restart until the heat exchanger has cooled significantly.
safetyWarning: CRITICAL: The heat exchanger is dangerously hot when this error occurs. Do not touch any metal surfaces. Allow at least 30 minutes for cooling before any inspection.

causes.cause: Severe scale buildup in heat exchanger
Very low or no water flow
Gas valve stuck at high fire

fixSteps.title: Allow Cooling
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the unit and wait at least 30 minutes for the heat exchanger to cool.

fixSteps.title: Check Water Flow
fixSteps.instruction: Verify all water valves are open and the inlet filter is clean.

fixSteps.title: Full Descale
fixSteps.instruction: Perform a thorough descaling flush for at least 60 minutes to remove scale buildup.

fixSteps.title: Professional Assessment
fixSteps.instruction: After descaling, have a technician verify heat exchanger integrity and check gas valve modulation.

whenToCallPro: If E5 has occurred multiple times or descaling does not help, professional inspection is needed to check for heat exchanger damage.

faq.q: Can E5 damage the heat exchanger permanently?
faq.a: Yes. Repeated overheating can cause cracking or warping, requiring expensive replacement.

faq.q: How much does a Rheem heat exchanger cost?
faq.a: Typically -,200 installed. Check if your unit is still under warranty.

title: Electrical Grounding Fault
shortDescription: The unit has detected an improper electrical grounding condition.
fullDescription:
Error E7 indicates the Rheem unit has detected an electrical grounding problem. Proper grounding is essential for the flame detection system and overall safe operation of the electronic controls.

The flame detection circuit uses electrical current flow through the flame to detect its presence. Without proper grounding, this detection circuit cannot function reliably, potentially causing ignition failures.

This error commonly occurs with improperly wired outlets, missing ground connections, or when using extension cords.
safetyWarning: Grounding issues can create shock hazards. Do not touch the unit with wet hands. Have an electrician verify proper grounding.

fixSteps.title: Test Outlet
fixSteps.instruction: Use an outlet tester to verify the outlet has proper grounding and correct polarity.

fixSteps.title: Remove Extension Cord
fixSteps.instruction: If using an extension cord, plug the unit directly into a properly grounded wall outlet.

fixSteps.title: Check Internal Ground
fixSteps.instruction: Open the front cover and verify the internal ground wire connection is secure.

fixSteps.title: Call Electrician
fixSteps.instruction: If the outlet is not properly grounded, have a licensed electrician install a grounded outlet.

whenToCallPro: If the outlet tests correctly but the error persists, call an electrician and a plumber.

faq.q: Can I use an extension cord with my Rheem?
faq.a: No. The unit should be plugged directly into a properly grounded outlet.

faq.q: What is reversed polarity?
faq.a: It means the hot and neutral wires are swapped at the outlet. An outlet tester can detect this quickly.

title: Fan Motor Failure
shortDescription: The combustion fan is not operating correctly.
fullDescription:
Error E9 indicates the combustion fan motor is not running at the correct speed or has failed entirely. The fan provides combustion air for safe and efficient operation, and its speed is monitored by the control board.

Fan failure can be caused by worn bearings, dust accumulation on the blades, electrical failure, or a wiring issue. Without proper fan operation, the unit cannot safely combust gas.

A failing fan often gives warning signs before complete failure, such as unusual humming, grinding, or vibration during operation.
safetyWarning: A malfunctioning combustion fan can lead to incomplete combustion and carbon monoxide production. Ensure CO detectors are functional.

fixSteps.title: Listen to Fan
fixSteps.instruction: When the unit attempts to start, listen for grinding, squealing, or abnormal vibration from the fan.

fixSteps.title: Check Fan Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Open the front cover and verify all fan motor connections are secure at both ends.

fixSteps.title: Inspect and Clean Fan
fixSteps.instruction: Check fan blades for dust buildup and clean with a soft brush if needed.

fixSteps.title: Replace Fan Motor
fixSteps.instruction: If the motor has failed, it must be replaced with an OEM Rheem fan motor assembly.

whenToCallPro: If the fan makes abnormal noise or does not spin, call a technician for motor replacement and combustion verification.

faq.q: How long do Rheem fan motors last?
faq.a: Typically 8-15 years depending on usage and environment.

faq.q: Can I oil the fan bearings?
faq.a: Most fan motors have sealed bearings that cannot be serviced. Replacement is the solution.

title: Inlet Temperature Sensor Fault
shortDescription: The cold water inlet temperature sensor has failed or is reading out of range.
fullDescription:
Error F1 indicates the cold water inlet temperature sensor (thermistor) is providing readings outside the acceptable range. The control board needs this sensor data to calculate the required temperature rise and modulate the burner accordingly.

The sensor may have failed open (infinite resistance), failed short (zero resistance), or have a corroded connection. Without accurate inlet temperature data, the unit cannot safely regulate output temperature.

This is typically a straightforward repair involving checking the connection and potentially replacing the sensor.

fixSteps.title: Check Sensor Connection
fixSteps.instruction: Open the front cover and verify the inlet temperature sensor connector is securely seated.

fixSteps.title: Measure Sensor Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the sensor and measure resistance with a multimeter. Check the service manual for expected values.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If resistance is out of specification, replace with an OEM Rheem inlet temperature sensor.

whenToCallPro: If sensor replacement does not clear the error, the control board input circuit may be at fault.

faq.q: Where is the inlet temperature sensor?
faq.a: On the cold water inlet pipe near the bottom of the unit.

faq.q: Can I use a generic thermistor?
faq.a: No. Use an OEM Rheem sensor to ensure proper resistance characteristics for your model.

title: Outlet Temperature Sensor Fault
shortDescription: The hot water outlet temperature sensor has failed or is reading out of range.
fullDescription:
Error F4 indicates the outlet water temperature sensor is providing abnormal readings. This sensor is critical for the temperature control loop, measuring the actual output temperature and comparing it to the set point.

Without this sensor, the unit cannot verify the water is being delivered at the correct temperature. The unit shuts down to prevent delivering water at an uncontrolled temperature, which could be dangerously hot.

The sensor may have failed due to age, heat exposure, or corroded connections.
safetyWarning: A failed outlet sensor could allow water to overheat. Do not bypass this sensor.

fixSteps.title: Check Sensor Connection
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the outlet temperature sensor connector is securely plugged into the control board.

fixSteps.title: Measure Sensor Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect and test with a multimeter. Compare to service manual specification.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If out of specification, replace with an OEM Rheem outlet temperature sensor.

fixSteps.title: Verify After Replacement
fixSteps.instruction: Run hot water and confirm the displayed temperature matches a thermometer reading.

whenToCallPro: If replacing the sensor does not clear the error, the control board may need professional diagnosis.

faq.q: Are the inlet and outlet sensors the same part?
faq.a: They may or may not be interchangeable. Order the correct part for each location.

faq.q: Can a sensor fail intermittently?
faq.a: Yes, a failing thermistor can give erratic readings before complete failure.

title: Exhaust Temperature Sensor Fault
shortDescription: The exhaust temperature sensor has failed or is reading out of range.
fullDescription:
Error F5 indicates the exhaust gas temperature sensor (thermistor) is providing readings outside the acceptable range. This sensor monitors exhaust temperatures to ensure combustion is operating safely and efficiently.

Without accurate exhaust temperature monitoring, the unit cannot detect dangerous combustion conditions such as heat exchanger overheating or blocked venting. The unit shuts down as a safety precaution.

The sensor is located in the exhaust path and is exposed to high temperatures and corrosive combustion gases, which can cause premature failure.
safetyWarning: Exhaust temperature monitoring is a critical safety function. Do not bypass this sensor. It protects against overheating and carbon monoxide risks.

causes.cause: Corroded sensor from exhaust gas exposure

fixSteps.title: Check Sensor Connection
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the exhaust temperature sensor connector is firmly seated at the control board.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Check the sensor for visible corrosion or damage from exhaust gas exposure.

fixSteps.title: Measure Sensor Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect and measure resistance. At room temperature, it should read approximately 10k-15k ohms (check manual).

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If out of range, replace with an OEM Rheem exhaust temperature sensor.

whenToCallPro: If replacing the sensor does not clear the error, a technician should check the control board and perform a combustion analysis.

faq.q: Why do exhaust sensors fail?
faq.a: Exposure to high temperatures and corrosive combustion gases causes degradation over time.

faq.q: Can I use a universal sensor?
faq.a: Use an OEM Rheem sensor to ensure correct resistance values for your model.

title: Flow Sensor Malfunction
shortDescription: The water flow sensor is not reading correctly or has failed.
fullDescription:
Error F7 indicates the water flow sensor is providing abnormal readings or no signal. The flow sensor tells the control board when water is flowing and at what rate, which triggers the burner to activate and determines the heat output.

A failed flow sensor prevents the unit from detecting water demand, so it will not fire. Mineral deposits on the turbine, a failed Hall effect sensor, or debris blocking the turbine are common causes.

In some cases, cleaning the flow sensor turbine can restore function, but if the electronic sensor has failed, replacement is needed.

causes.cause: Mineral deposits on flow sensor turbine

symptoms: Unit does not activate when hot water is opened

fixSteps.title: Check Sensor Connection
fixSteps.instruction: Open the front cover and verify the flow sensor cable is securely connected to the control board.

fixSteps.title: Clean Flow Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the flow sensor and clean the turbine of mineral deposits and debris. Soak in vinegar if needed.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Reinstall the sensor, open a hot water faucet, and check if the unit detects flow.

fixSteps.title: Replace Flow Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If cleaning does not work, replace the flow sensor with an OEM Rheem part.

whenToCallPro: If sensor replacement does not resolve the issue, a technician should check the control board.

faq.q: How does the flow sensor work?
faq.a: A turbine spins with water flow, and a Hall effect sensor counts rotations to determine flow rate.

faq.q: How much does a Rheem flow sensor cost?
faq.a: Typically - for the OEM part.

title: Exhaust Blockage Detected
shortDescription: The unit has detected a blockage in the exhaust venting system.
fullDescription:
Error F12 indicates the Rheem unit has detected a significant blockage or restriction in the exhaust venting system. The air pressure sensor or fan current sensing has detected conditions that indicate the exhaust cannot flow freely.

Common causes include ice formation at the vent termination in winter, bird or insect nests, disconnected vent joints, or improper vent installation with excessive length or elbows.

This is a critical safety error because a blocked exhaust can force carbon monoxide back into the living space.
safetyWarning: CRITICAL: A blocked exhaust vent can cause deadly carbon monoxide to enter your home. Ensure CO detectors are working. If a CO alarm sounds, evacuate immediately and call 911.

causes.cause: Ice or snow at vent termination
Bird or insect nest in vent

fixSteps.title: Check Vent Termination
fixSteps.instruction: Go outside and inspect the vent termination for ice, snow, nests, or debris. Clear any blockages.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Vent Pipe
fixSteps.instruction: Check the entire vent run for disconnections, sags, or crushed sections.

fixSteps.title: Check Bird Screen
fixSteps.instruction: If the vent has a bird screen, ensure it is not clogged with debris.

fixSteps.title: Reset and Test
fixSteps.instruction: After clearing the blockage, reset the unit and run hot water to verify operation.

whenToCallPro: If the vent appears clear but the error persists, a technician should check the air pressure sensor and vent installation.

faq.q: Why does F12 happen more in winter?
faq.a: Condensate in the exhaust can freeze at the vent termination when temperatures drop below freezing.

faq.q: Can I extend my vent to prevent ice?
faq.a: A wind-resistant or anti-freeze vent cap is usually better than extending the vent, which may exceed maximum length.

title: Air Supply Blockage
shortDescription: The combustion air intake is blocked or restricted.
fullDescription:
Error F13 indicates the combustion air supply to the unit is blocked or restricted. On direct-vent and sealed combustion models, air is drawn through a dedicated intake pipe. If this pipe is blocked, the unit cannot get enough air for safe combustion.

This error can also occur if the air intake filter (present on some models) is clogged with dust, lint, or debris. In attic or garage installations, insulation or storage materials may have been placed against the air intake.

Proper combustion air supply is as important as proper exhaust venting for safe operation.
safetyWarning: Insufficient combustion air can lead to incomplete combustion and carbon monoxide production. Ensure CO detectors are working properly.

fixSteps.title: Check Air Intake Termination
fixSteps.instruction: Go outside and verify the air intake vent is clear of debris, leaves, or ice.

fixSteps.title: Clear Area Around Unit
fixSteps.instruction: Remove any objects, insulation, or materials placed near the air intake on the unit.

fixSteps.title: Clean Air Filter
fixSteps.instruction: If your model has an air intake filter, remove and clean it or replace if heavily soiled.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Air Intake Pipe
fixSteps.instruction: Check the air intake pipe for disconnections or blockages along its length.

whenToCallPro: If the intake appears clear but the error persists, a technician should check the pressure sensor and combustion system.

faq.q: Does my unit have a separate air intake?
faq.a: Direct-vent models have separate intake and exhaust pipes. Power-vent models draw air from the installation space.

faq.q: How much clearance does the air intake need?
faq.a: At least 12 inches of clearance from any obstruction. Check your installation manual for specific requirements.

title: Water Leak Detected
shortDescription: The internal leak sensor has detected water inside the unit.
fullDescription:
Error F16 indicates the water leak sensor inside the Rheem unit has detected water where it should not be present. This means there is an internal leak from a connection, seal, heat exchanger, or other water-carrying component.

Internal leaks can be caused by loose fittings, failed o-rings, corroded connections, or a cracked heat exchanger. Freeze damage is also a common cause if the unit lost power during freezing weather.

This error should be addressed immediately to prevent water damage to the unit electronic components and the surrounding area.
safetyWarning: Turn off the water supply to the unit immediately. If the unit is gas-powered, turn off the gas supply as well to prevent any interaction between water and gas components.

fixSteps.title: Shut Off Water and Gas
fixSteps.instruction: Immediately close the water isolation valves and gas supply to the unit.

fixSteps.title: Identify Leak Source
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the front panel, place towels underneath, and slowly open water to identify the leak location.

fixSteps.title: Tighten Connections
fixSteps.instruction: If the leak is from a fitting, tighten it. If from a gasket or o-ring, replace it.

fixSteps.title: Heat Exchanger Leak
fixSteps.instruction: If the heat exchanger is leaking, professional replacement is required.

whenToCallPro: If the leak is from the heat exchanger or the source cannot be identified, call a Rheem-certified technician.

faq.q: Does Rheem warranty cover heat exchanger leaks?
faq.a: Most Rheem tankless models have a 12-year heat exchanger warranty when properly registered.

faq.q: Can freeze damage cause leaks?
faq.a: Yes. Water expands when it freezes and can crack the heat exchanger, fittings, and internal plumbing.

title: Control Board / PCB Error
shortDescription: The main control board has detected an internal fault.
fullDescription:
Error F17 indicates the main PCB (printed circuit board) has detected an internal diagnostic failure that prevents normal operation. The control board manages all unit functions including sensors, gas valve, fan, and ignition.

Control board failures can be caused by power surges, moisture intrusion, component aging, or manufacturing defects. A power surge from a lightning strike or utility event is one of the most common causes.

This error typically requires control board replacement, which is a significant repair. If the unit is under warranty, the board may be covered.

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the unit and disconnect power for 60 seconds, then reconnect. Some transient errors may clear.

fixSteps.title: Inspect for Moisture
fixSteps.instruction: Open the front cover and check the control board for moisture, condensation, or corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Check All Connections
fixSteps.instruction: Verify all wire harness connections to the control board are secure and free of corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Replace Control Board
fixSteps.instruction: If the error persists, the control board must be replaced by a certified technician.

whenToCallPro: If a power cycle does not clear F17, a certified technician is needed for control board replacement.

faq.q: How much does a Rheem control board cost?
faq.a: Control boards typically cost $200-$450 for the part, depending on the model.

faq.q: Should I install a surge protector?
faq.a: Yes, a surge protector is highly recommended for all tankless water heaters to protect the control board.

faq.q: Is the control board under warranty?
faq.a: Most Rheem models have a 5-year parts warranty. Check your registration for details.
